purisanāgo in the Buddha epithet-string (Paṭisambhidāmagga); polysemy

note: nāgarājā with phaṇa/bhoga; serpent-being acting
nāgamaṃsu/nāgamessasi (na+āgam-) 'did not/ will not come'; nānā-gabbhā 'various chambers'
nāgamessanti/nāgamethā (na+āgam-) 'would not come'; mahānāmo/mahānāge are the Sakyan Mahānāma, not nāga
ārāmaṃ nāgamaṃsu 'did not come to the monastery'; na+āgam-

claim: the fierce fire-room nāgarāja is iddhimā, āsiviso, ghoraviso (potent, venomous serpent); the Buddha subdues it
note: Uruvelakassapa episode; ahināga, nāgayoni, manussanāga elsewhere in passage
hatthināgo crowded by hatthī/hatthinī, eats grass, drinks water; bull-elephant (Kosambī, Pālileyya frame)

claim: a nāga dwelling at the Mandākinī lotus-pond gives Moggallāna lotus-stalks and commands another nāga
note: nāga in water, nāgarāja; serpent-being interacting
nāgamesuṃ/nāgamentānaṃ (na+āgam-) 'did not come'; vāhanāgāresu = vāhana+āgāra (carriage-sheds)
ekaṃ samathaṃ anāgamma 'without resorting to one settlement'; an-āgamma
nāgadantaka/nāgadante = ivory peg on the wall for hanging bowls; nānā-gottā incidental
nāgā/mahānāgā at a lake using soṇḍā (trunks) to pull lotus-roots; bhiṅkacchāpā = young; bull-elephants
chedanāgaṇḍa in an uddāna mnemonic = chedana+gaṇḍa; not nāga

claim: the great ocean is the abode of great beings — timi, asurā, nāgā, gandhabbā — with bodies up to many leagues
note: nāgā among the sea-dwelling bhūtā; ontological list of beings
cūḷanāgo/mahānāgo/nāgatthero = elder teachers in the vinaya-lineage roll-call; nāgamesi=na+āgam-
vatthu-title Erakapatta-nāgarāja-vatthu (the nāga-king Erakapatta); story-frame label, verse itself general

window = anāgāro paribbaje 'homeless one' (an-agāra); nāgavagge is the chapter label only
bandhanāgāre/bandhanāgārā = bandhana+āgāra 'prison'; not nāga

claim: a nāga-king venerates a relic-share at Rāmagāma and one of the Buddha's tooth-relics; nāga-kings hold/honor relics
note: nāgarājā maheti (twice); devinda-nāginda-narinda-pūjito; nāgāpalokita is the 'elephant-look' gaze, separate sense

rañño ārohaṇīyaṃ nāgaṃ abhiruhitvā — the king mounts his riding elephant; royal elephant

claim: Mahāsamaya: nāgas (Nāgasā, Vesālā, Tacchakā, Kambala-Assatara, Yāmunā, Dhataraṭṭhā, Erāvaṇa) come to the assembly; supaṇṇas threaten them and the Buddha makes peace; nāgas take refuge
note: nāgarāja, nāga-vs-supaṇṇa antagonism; serpent-being class as named lineages
devā manussā asurā nāgā gandhabbā — nāgas in a stock list of being-classes; passing
nāgehi āviñcheyyāma 'drag him with nāgas' — could be elephants (drag with elephants) or serpents; window cannot fix
nānā-gottā 'of various clans' (nānājaccā nānānāmā nānāgottā); not nāga
Lakkhaṇa verse: gandhabba-nāgā, devā manussa-asura-sakka-rakkhasā, nāgā among being-classes who attend; passing

claim: Āṭānāṭiya: nāgo/nāgī/nāgapotako/nāgamahāmatto/nāgapārisajja — nāgas have a society (males, females, young, ministers, courtiers) parallel to gandhabbas/kumbhaṇḍas
note: nāga as a structured class of non-human beings
Dubbalakaṭṭha: 'tassa ce bhāyasī nāga' — nāga as address; here a tree-spirit/being addressed as nāga, contextually an honorific address
nāgasirivhayano in uddāna = the Jātaka named after Nāgasiri (a personal name); roll-call mnemonic
nāguṭṭhi-gaṇanaṃ = nan̄guṭṭha/nāguṭṭha (plough) counting; agricultural term, not nāga the being

claim: a nāga ferries a faithful lay-devotee across by boat (Nāgo nāvāya vaṇṇena saddhaṃ vahatupāsakaṃ) — fruit of faith/virtue
note: Sīlānisaṃsa; nāga acts as helper, nāgavarena in uddāna
Gūthapāṇa: dung-beetle challenges; 'ehi nāga nivattassu', reply 'na pādā na dantehi na soṇḍiyā' (feet/tusks/trunk) = elephant
mā bhāyi nāgamissatī = na+āgamissati 'he will not come'; sandhi false friend

claim: a nāga owns a wish-granting gem (maṇi); when a brahmin begs it repeatedly the nāga withdraws and never reappears
note: Maṇikaṇṭha Jātaka; nāga as gem-bearing serpent-being
Mudupāṇi: 'nāgo cassa sukārito' — a well-trained nāga (parallel to a soft hand); a tamed elephant
Abhiṇha: nāgo snehamakāsi kukkure — the elephant grew fond of the dog; kabaḷa (fodder-ball), ghaṃsituṃ; elephant
nāgasirivhayano in uddāna = Jātaka named after Nāgasiri; mnemonic roll-call
Garudhamma: 'dadāmi vo brāhmaṇā nāgametaṃ, rājārahaṃ rājabhoggaṃ... alaṅkataṃ hemajālābhichannaṃ sasārathiṃ' — a royal caparisoned elephant with a driver

claim: even a venomous nāga (ghoraviso nāgo) is not killed because it is virtuous (sīlavā) — virtue protects
note: Sīlavīmaṃsaka; ghoraviso = venom marks serpent

claim: a venomous nāga (ghoraviso nāgo), being virtuous, is not slain — virtue spares it
note: Sīlavīmaṃsa; same ghoraviso serpent topos as ja290/ja86
Jambuka: the jackal attacks the lion-like one and is killed 'nāgena hato' — by the elephant; sañjātakhandha/mahabbala context
ṭhitaṃ maṃ nāgamissati = na+āgamissati 'will not come to me'; sandhi false friend

claim: a nāga-king is destroyed by weak creatures (crow, frog, fly) banding together — even a mighty nāga falls to enmity
note: Laṭukika; nāgarājā, nāgaṃ aghātesuṃ; serpent-being slain

claim: a malicious nāga seizes a boat in the Ganges current and a being rescues the men from it
note: Baka Jātaka; luddena nāgena (cruel nāga) in water; serpent
Kosambiya: eko care mātaṅgaraññeva nāgo — wander alone like the elephant in the mātaṅga forest; explicit mātaṅga

claim: the nāga-king (with garuḷa/venateyya, gandhabba-king, the Kuru king) declares his virtue is khanti (patience) — nāga as one of four exemplary kings
note: Catuposathiya; nāgarājā paired against garuḷa, the canonical serpent-vs-supaṇṇa pairing
Mātuposaka: the nāga eats a fodder-ball (kabaḷa), supports his blind mother; nāgarājaṃ/mahānāga = the great bull-elephant who feeds his mother
sirimātusuposakanāgavaro in uddāna compounds Jātaka-title mnemonics (Sirimanta, Mātuposaka, Nāga...); roll-call label

claim: a hooded nāga (nāgo jātaphaṇo), mighty, caught the falling prince in its coils and saved him in a mountain abyss
note: Mahāpaduma; jātaphaṇo (with hood), bhogehi (coils) = serpent
Kāliṅgabodhi: noble nāgas (nāgā abhijātā ca kuñjarā) cannot tread the bodhimaṇḍa; abhijāto nāgo / kuñjaraṃ dantiṃ; tusker elephants
Bhisa identification list: Pālileyyo tadā nāgo — Pālileyya was then the (Pārileyyaka) elephant

claim: nāgas emerge fully armed (sannaddhā paṇḍavīsati) with archers to attack tree-fellers — a nāga host guarding/avenging
note: Mahāvāṇija; nāgā nikkhamiṃsu as a martial host of serpent-beings (tree-deity/nāga retinue)
Cittasambhūta: 'kacchaṃ nāgānaṃ bandhatha' — gird the elephants (for the royal chariots/yoke); harnessing draft elephants
nānā-gottā vanibbakā 'beggars of various clans'; nāge here is na+... in verse, not the being; window shows nānāgottā
Sirīmanta gift-list: 'gavaṃ sahassaṃ usabhañca nāgaṃ, ājaññayutte ca rathe' — a bull and an elephant among gifts; elephant

claim: Campeyya the nāga-king keeps uposatha as a serpent (uraga), is captured by a snake-charmer, freed, and invites the king to his nāga palace; bodhisatta-as-nāga
note: Campeyya Jātaka; nāgakaññā, nāgakula, nāgabhavana, uraga, ossaṭṭhakāyo; serpent-being, bodhisatta
Hatthipāla: 'nāgo chetvāva bandhanaṃ' — like an elephant snapping its tether the king went forth; elephant simile
Kiṃchanda river-description: bahukā nāgavittodā among vanatodā/dumagaṇā (groves of trees/flowering plants); nāga = ironwood tree context
Chaddanta: the six-tusked elephant-king; dante chinda, gajuttamassa, hatthināgo, aṭṭhasahassanāgā, nāgavadhena; bull-elephant

claim: the nāga-king Paṇḍaraka is seized by the supaṇṇa once his secret is betrayed (Nāgaṃ yathā paṇḍarakaṃ supaṇṇo)
note: Paṇḍaranāgarāja Jātaka; nāga-vs-supaṇṇa predation; serpent-king
paṇḍaranāgavaro in uddāna = mnemonic for the Paṇḍara-nāga(-rāja) Jātaka; story-title label for a serpent tale
bahussutaṃ anāgamma 'not having resorted to the learned'; an-āgamma sandhi

claim: Saṅkhapāla the nāga-king keeps uposatha enduring torment by hunters, longing to be reborn human (manussayoniṃ abhipatthayāno); bodhisatta-as-nāga
note: Saṅkhapāla Jātaka; nāgarāja, uposatha, deviddhi, wishes the human realm superior; bodhisatta
Cūḷasutasoma: 'Nāgova ekako carati' — like an elephant he wanders alone (renunciant simile)
Soṇaka: saṭṭhi/saṭṭhi nāgasahassāni, suvaṇṇakacchā mātaṅgā (gold-girthed tuskers), tomaraṅkusapāṇi (goad-bearers); war-elephants
Saṅkicca hell-description simile: 'nāgo tuttaṭṭito yathā' — like an elephant tormented by the goad (tutta); elephant
Kusa: nāganāsūrū 'elephant-trunk thighs' epithet of Pabhāvatī; nāgasahassāni; elephant
Sudhābhojana: a thousand horses 'susunāgasādise' (like young elephants) yoked to the chariot; elephant comparison
Kuṇāla: nāga(-rukkha) inside a long compound of flowering trees (...campaka-asoka-nāgarukkha...); ironwood tree in a flora list
Mahāsutasoma: 'hayehi nāgehi rathehupetaṃ' — a city furnished with horses, elephants, chariots; elephant
Mūgapakkha: 'kacchaṃ nāgāna bandhatha' — gird the elephants (yoke for the procession); draft elephants
Mahājanaka: 'nāgo dantehi haññate' — the elephant is killed for its tusks (danta); elephant
Suvaṇṇasāma: the archer-king boasts 'nāgopi me na mucceyya' — not even an elephant would escape my arrow; game-elephant
anāgārā tapassino 'homeless ascetics' (an-agāra); not nāga
Umaṅga war-scene: mahānāgā/nāgā/nāgakhandhe (elephant-backs), nāganāsūru; tuskers in battle (Nāgā ca kappitā dantī balavanto)

claim: Bhūridatta the nāga: a snake-charmer threatens 'alampāno hi nāgena' and boasts of mastering venomless/venomous nāgas; the nāga is the bodhisatta serpent
note: Bhūridatta Jātaka; avisena ca nāgena, uragaṃ; serpent-being, bodhisatta-as-nāga

claim: Vidhura: the nāga realm with nāga-king/queen (nāgarājā, nāgi), nāga-maidens (nāgakaññā), nāga-kingdom (nāgakula); a nāgi practising non-malice attains the deva-world
note: Vidhura Jātaka; full nāga court; nāgakaññā, nāgādhipati; serpent-beings
Vessantara: the gift of the state elephant — pavaraṃ nāgaṃ īsādantaṃ, hatthināge padinnamhi, pabhinnaṃ kuñjaraṃ; the rain-bringing royal tusker; punnāgā/giripunnāgā are trees, incidental

claim: a venomous nāga (ghoraviso nāgo), being virtuous, is not killed — virtue protects it
note: Sīlavīmaṃsaka; ghoraviso = venom marks serpent (same topos as ja290/ja330)

claim: Nidhikaṇḍa: nāgas can spirit away a buried treasure (Nāgā vā apanāmenti), as yakkhas carry it off
note: nāgas paired with yakkhas as treasure-removing beings; serpent power over hidden wealth
the monk Nāgasena (Milinda, Pubbayoga); nāgānubaddho = following Nāga(sena)
āyasmā nāgaseno — the elder Nāgasena answering King Milinda

window narrates Devadatta/bodhisatta past lives addressed to Nāgasena; hatthināgo (elephant) in narrative, frame = the monk
window is Bhante nāgasena Q&A on arahants' fearlessness; hatthi (Dhanapālaka) is an elephant, frame = the monk
window frames Bhante nāgasena; quotes Chaddanta nāgarājā (serpent? elephant-king Chaddanta), but coded token = the monk dominating window
window is Bhante nāgasena praise/Q&A on water-life; hatthināgānaṃ token elsewhere
frame Bhante nāgasena; the listed nāgā supaṇṇā (serpent class) sits in an enumeration of beings, but window subject = the monk
window is Bhante nāgasena dialogue on the ten non-gifts; nāgabhavane/uposathanāgarāja tokens elsewhere
window frames Bhante nāgasena; the hatthināga (elephant) is the simile-vehicle in the narrative, subject = the monk
āyasmā nāgaseno / bhante nāgasena throughout (Anumānapañha); the elder Nāgasena
Bhante nāgasena Q&A on dhutaṅgas; dhanapālahatthināga = the elephant Dhanapāla in narrative, frame = the monk
Bhante nāgasena frame; quoted Dhaniya verse nāgo pūtilataṃ = elephant simile-vehicle (parallel to kn18_5)
Bhante nāgasena dialogue; anāgārehi (an-agāra 'homeless') in the quoted verse, frame = the monk
Dantabhūmi: rañño nāgaṃ, nāgavanaṃ, nāgavanika (elephant-hunter), āraññakaṃ nāgaṃ; the king's elephant tamed; explicit royal-elephant frame
Upakkilesa: eko care mātaṅgaraññeva nāgo — wander alone like the elephant in the mātaṅga forest

claim: Vammika riddle: digging the anthill one finds a nāga; 'leave the nāga, do not strike it, pay homage' — the nāga = the arahant (allegory), literal referent a serpent in the mound
note: nāgo found in the anthill (vammika); the riddle's literal nāga is a serpent, glossed as the khīṇāsava
